video_title: HOLY SH*T!! New Data Shows The Recession Actually Start 8 Months Ago!?
transcript: Did a recession begin in the United States in October of 2025? Not just the forgot how to grow style that we've been grappling with for the last couple years, but a fullblown allout NBR style recession. Take a look at the data and charts that we'll show you in this video. Let's see what you think about all this. And keep in mind, the numbers that we're going to be going through and focusing on are those that the NBER itself uses when deciding the business cycle question. What makes these numbers particularly powerful and compelling is both what they're showing and where they come from. We're talking about private sector income, the most essential part of every economy. This is why the NBR itself looks to this specific data when it comes time to decide the recession question. Now, we've been talking about and documenting job losses for months now. Flat beverage. The data showing all of that comes from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. These income estimates come from a completely separate agency, the Bureau of Economic Analysis. They're looking at the same thing, but from a completely different perspective, yet arriving at the same place, the BLS data already did. In other words, some of the most critical macroeconomic evidence that we have agrees something big changed late last year and changed in a way that looks an awful lot like previous business cycle peaks. There is this growing sense and growing evidence for the classic historical energy shock case. And like I said, this is data that started long before we got to March in the Iran conflict. Steve, you saw the numbers as I did. We have income data that came out, GDP, the savings rate, you know, personal income and spending from the Bureau of Economic Analysis. What's most compelling about it to me, you know, we've been talking about the establishment survey and the household survey, the BLS labor data for quite some time. The BEA is completely separate outfit. They track income from an economy perspective and they say, "You know what? It kind of looks like a recession here." Yeah, Jeeoff. And this is like the clearest sign of a K-shaped economy that we've had yet. For for some Americans, very clearly, they're in a recession. They don't have enough money. Prices are going up. They're struggling. They're looking at the labor market. If they're trying to get more hours or maybe even a part-time job, the opportunities aren't there. And yet, for others, they're saying, "Well, wait a minute. the stock market's up, you know, I'm getting huge bonuses, I'm outspending money. I don't see it. And that's one of the challenges here, particularly as you go into energy shocks, is it doesn't affect everybody across the board. You start to see it happen at the bottom. And then, you know, what do we notice? You hear from retailers like Walmart saying, "Hey, you know, business is starting to boom here, except all of a sudden people are getting very mindful how much gas they purchase." And we heard from Home Depot and Lowe's are saying, "Wait a minute, people all of a sudden in April cut back their spending." So we start to see more and more evidence that incomes really do matter here. And as you said, the evidence is the savings rate. You're talking about what's been really propping up the economy, tax refund checks, and people dipping into their savings. The challenge is at some point there is a line in the sand where people say, "Look, my savings have now dropped to a certain level. I have to cut my spending." And next thing you know, we start to see that K-shaped economy start to collapse together. >> Yeah. The K-shaped economy, what really happens is the bottom part of that K-shape gets bigger and bigger and bigger as more people fall out of the top and into the bottom, which is what a lot of Americans either are feeling or they're afraid of. That's one reason why consumer sentiment is so bad because consumers are worried. They think, "Okay, maybe I'm in the upper part of the K today and things are looking looking all right, but if if this continues to go like it is, I'm afraid I'm going to end up in the bottom part and then what am I going to do?" You're you're absolutely right, Steve. The personal savings rate fell to 2.6%. One of the reasons it fell to 2.6% because, as we started the show with, income was terrible. Income was absolutely terrible. The topline income, personal income in nominal terms was zero zero growth rate in April. Now some of that had to do with adjustment and some of the data and details but we have seen personal income in nominal terms weaken over the last six seven eight months which is right around the time that the you know flat beverage job loss economy showed up to begin with. So once again we have the income data from the Bureau of Economic Analysis saying you know what the job losses they weren't huge job losses but the you know somewhat of job losses that were taking place over here in the Bureau of Labor Statistics we're seeing the same things from a different perspective and then it just goes from there. If nominal incomes are dropping they're not crashing they're not falling off a cliff but they are defin there's definitely an inflection and shift going on here. If nominal incomes are dropping, as we know, consumers don't respond to that right away. Like you said, Steve, they use their savings to use other funds, in this case, the tax refunds to, you know, they pull back a little bit, but not nearly as much as incomes pull back. And so spending starts to lag incomes, which means the savings rate just absolutely tumbles, which is exactly what the data showed. The savings rate for April was down to 2.6%, which is the lowest since 2022. Back in 2022, savings were low because prices were assuming soaring ahead so much more. These are numbers that you usually see in the early stages of a recession. So the savings rate like the income data, like the jobs data suggests something big shifted, not in March and April with the energy shock, but way back in 2025, we were talking about this. At the time it looked like, hey, something big is going on in the economy. And now we're getting more and more confirmation that that really was the case. When the NBER gets around there, you know, the business cycle dating committee, when they start thinking about recessions, this is one of the first numbers that they pull out. And what it has shown is a contraction in income, price adjusted income, because it's just like Steve said, incomes are flat on nominal terms. Maybe it's a little bit positive, but when you adjust for prices, consumers are falling further and further behind, which is exactly what the data shows, and it's exactly what the data shows, entering recessionary periods. The six-month change in real personal income excluding transfer receipts was minus0.9%. It's actually a little bit worse when you go back seven months. It's almost 1.2%. Now, that doesn't sound like a lot. You think, well, minus 1%, but we're talking economywide for just the private private income. And the only times you ever see a roughly minus 1% on a six-month basis is in periods of either recession or some big tax change where everybody has to frontload their payments into a year and then the next year incomes fall. This is not it's clearly not the case. We didn't see a tax increase in 2025 that frontloaded a bunch of income. So we have the economic case where incomes are falling off fast enough it looks like a recession. I mean just look at the chart on the screen. You know, and speaking of bottom falling out, if you look at the price of oil, you all of a sudden we see it dropping, you know, out of nowhere. And yet there's no signs that, you know, the conflict has really come to an end or there's going to be a huge surge in a lot of oil moving through the pipelines or around the global economy. So again, talk about confirmation of the data saying that consumers are pulling back and demand for oil and energy is dropping and that's a huge red flag. The reality is they can't afford it. So, as we look around, we're seeing all these confirmations that as you said there, you know, the lower income group of the country, they're definitely clearly in a recession. The problem is it's slowly filtering up into the middle class. And the question then becomes if it gets into the upper class where their spending starts to get hit, well, watch out that K as you said, it starts to widen.
